forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,6 @@
+---
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: 9f80
+title: Full swift test suite still hangs (cumulative, unlocated) after fixing 2 confirmed leaks
+---
+## What
After fixing two confirmed, verified concurrency bugs this session (see `^332rkv2`: ConnectionTests' skipped `close()` on throw; and UpdateMailbox's actor/onCancel scheduling gap, fixed as part of `^a2pbwvz`'s still-uncommitted working tree), the full `swift test` suite **still hangs** on every attempted full run, but now much later and in a different, unrelated location each time.

## Evidence gathered
- 3x full runs at 300s timeout: hung at 419/~420 (right after ConnectionTests' own known flaky-timeout failure), 387/~420 (TSCallGraphTests), 389/~420 (CallGraphOpsTests/BlastRadiusOps area).
- 1x full run at 1200s (patient, no other timeout pressure): 388 tests passed, then hung — confirmed via two CPU-time samples 2 minutes apart showing **zero** change (0:07.36 both times) — genuinely stalled, not slow.
- 1x full run with `swift test --parallel --num-workers 1` at 900s: still hung, this time at 391/~420, in the StoreTests/SearchCorpus area. This suggests `--num-workers` (a SwiftPM multi-process/bundle-parallelism knob) does not control swift-testing's own internal per-test task concurrency within a single process, so it wasn't a useful lever here.
- Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps taken at two different hang points (once mid-CallGraphOpsTests-area, once mid-StoreTests-area) show the **identical signature**: only 1-2 threads alive total. Main thread parked in `mach_msg`/`CFRunLoopRun` (swift-testing's async-main driver loop, waiting for the top-level task to finish). One `libdispatch` worker thread parked in `__workq_kernreturn` (idle, waiting for work). **Nothing is blocked in a syscall, nothing is running** — this is not resource/thread-pool exhaustion via blocked reads (that was the ConnectionTests bug's signature: many threads alive, each pinned in a blocking `read(2)`). This is a genuinely un-resumed `CheckedContinuation`/`TaskGroup` child somewhere, causing the whole async-main task (and hence the entire remaining test run) to suspend forever.
- Never reproduces in any isolated suite: `DiagnosticsTests` alone (5/5 clean runs), `ConnectionTests` alone (repeated clean runs including the new regression test). Only manifests after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the *same process*, and the exact location varies run to run — points to something cumulative/pervasive across many suites rather than one bad file.
- Grepped the entire `Sources/` tree for the same `actor` + detached-`Task`-inside-`onCancel` anti-pattern that caused the `UpdateMailbox` bug (see `^332rkv2`) — `UpdateMailbox` was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern.
- No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to compare against — this environment is Swift 6.4 / Xcode-beta.app targeting macOS 27.0 (26A5368g), all beta software. A toolchain-level Swift Concurrency runtime bug is a live possibility, not yet ruled out or in.
- Machine is persistently under heavy, unrelated load (load average 9-14 throughout this investigation, from other processes: multiple `rust-analyzer` instances, several `node` processes) — ruled out as the *sole* cause (CPU time on the hung process genuinely stops advancing, which pure contention/slowness would not produce), but can't be ruled out as a contributing/triggering factor for whatever the real bug is.

## Suggested next steps for whoever picks this up
- Consider a Thread-Sanitizer-instrumented build (`swift test --sanitize=thread`) if TSan support exists for this toolchain/platform — it's designed to catch exactly this class of bug and would likely pinpoint the exact unresolved continuation directly, far faster than further manual bisection.
- Bisection by suite subset (e.g. running the first N of ~30 suites, then N+5, etc.) to find the smallest reproducing combination, keeping in mind it may require several hundred tests' worth of *some* resource before manifesting (so small subsets may never reproduce it even if they contain the culprit).
- Audit every other `withTaskCancellationHandler`/`withCheckedContinuation`/`withTaskGroup` usage across `Sources/` and `Tests/Support/` (not just the `onCancel`-spawns-a-`Task` pattern already ruled out) for other structured-concurrency gaps.
- Try reproducing on a stable (non-beta) toolchain if one becomes available, to rule in/out a beta runtime bug.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full `swift test` completes reliably (multiple consecutive clean runs) without hanging
- [ ] Root cause identified and documented, not just papered over
- [ ] A regression test added if the root cause is in this package's own code (not the toolchain)

Filed from within task `^332rkv2`'s investigation; discovered while trying to verify tasks `^es8qv65` and `^a2pbwvz`'s work was safe to build on. Blocks confident full-suite verification for the rest of the `/finish` batch — the two isolated suites known to be involved in this session's other work (`DiagnosticsTests`, `ConnectionTests`) both pass reliably alone, so this is not blocking those two tasks specifically, but it does mean **no one should trust a full `swift test` run to actually complete** until this is resolved.
\ No newline at end of file

--- original
+++ modified
@@ -13,6 +13,19 @@

     Investigating `outOfOrderResponsesAreMatchedByIDNotArrivalOrder` in ConnectionTests.swift now — this is a NEW candidate distinct from the already-fixed close()-on-throw bug (per task constraints, will not touch already-fixed patterns unless a genuine new bug is found here).
   timestamp: 2026-07-05T21:47:26.012202+00:00
+- actor: wballard
+  id: 01kwvjvyyx28c6gztvja3ppwp4
+  text: |-
+    Root cause identified via manual audit of `Sources/FoundationModelsCodeContext/LSP/ProcessLanguageServerConnection.swift` (the TSan run's earliest-hanging test, `outOfOrderResponsesAreMatchedByIDNotArrivalOrder` in ConnectionTests.swift, pointed here — see prior comment).
+
+    **Bug**: `performRequest` writes a request to the child process's stdin *before* `awaitResponse(id:)` ever registers a `CheckedContinuation` for that request's id in `PendingRequestTable` (the write is synchronous; registration happens inside a separately-scheduled `withThrowingTaskGroup` child task). Nothing enforces that registration wins the race against a real round trip. `route(message:...)` called `pendingRequests.resolve(id:with:)` and ignored its `Bool` return — so if a fast response arrived before the continuation registered, it was silently discarded. The registration that followed was then orphaned: only its own sibling timeout task (a real 30s `ContinuousClock` sleep by default) could ever resume it, and if that timeout task's own wakeup was starved by the same cooperative-thread-pool pressure that lost the original race (exactly what several hundred concurrently-running tests, or TSan's much heavier per-op overhead, create), the continuation — and the whole `Task` awaiting it — hung forever with zero CPU activity. This exactly matches the task's thread-sample evidence (main thread parked in mach_msg/CFRunLoop, one dispatch worker idle in __workq_kernreturn, nothing blocked in a syscall).
+

 title: Full swift test suite still hangs (cumulative, unlocated) after fixing 2 confirmed leaks
 ---
-## What
After fixing two confirmed, verified concurrency bugs this session (see `^332rkv2`: ConnectionTests' skipped `close()` on throw; and UpdateMailbox's actor/onCancel scheduling gap, fixed as part of `^a2pbwvz`'s still-uncommitted working tree), the full `swift test` suite **still hangs** on every attempted full run, but now much later and in a different, unrelated location each time.

## Evidence gathered
- 3x full runs at 300s timeout: hung at 419/~420 (right after ConnectionTests' own known flaky-timeout failure), 387/~420 (TSCallGraphTests), 389/~420 (CallGraphOpsTests/BlastRadiusOps area).
- 1x full run at 1200s (patient, no other timeout pressure): 388 tests passed, then hung — confirmed via two CPU-time samples 2 minutes apart showing **zero** change (0:07.36 both times) — genuinely stalled, not slow.
- 1x full run with `swift test --parallel --num-workers 1` at 900s: still hung, this time at 391/~420, in the StoreTests/SearchCorpus area. This suggests `--num-workers` (a SwiftPM multi-process/bundle-parallelism knob) does not control swift-testing's own internal per-test task concurrency within a single process, so it wasn't a useful lever here.
- Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps taken at two different hang points (once mid-CallGraphOpsTests-area, once mid-StoreTests-area) show the **identical signature**: only 1-2 threads alive total. Main thread parked in `mach_msg`/`CFRunLoopRun` (swift-testing's async-main driver loop, waiting for the top-level task to finish). One `libdispatch` worker thread parked in `__workq_kernreturn` (idle, waiting for work). **Nothing is blocked in a syscall, nothing is running** — this is not resource/thread-pool exhaustion via blocked reads (that was the ConnectionTests bug's signature: many threads alive, each pinned in a blocking `read(2)`). This is a genuinely un-resumed `CheckedContinuation`/`TaskGroup` child somewhere, causing the whole async-main task (and hence the entire remaining test run) to suspend forever.
- Never reproduces in any isolated suite: `DiagnosticsTests` alone (5/5 clean runs), `ConnectionTests` alone (repeated clean runs including the new regression test). Only manifests after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the *same process*, and the exact location varies run to run — points to something cumulative/pervasive across many suites rather than one bad file.
- Grepped the entire `Sources/` tree for the same `actor` + detached-`Task`-inside-`onCancel` anti-pattern that caused the `UpdateMailbox` bug (see `^332rkv2`) — `UpdateMailbox` was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern.
- No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to compare against — this environment is Swift 6.4 / Xcode-beta.app targeting macOS 27.0 (26A5368g), all beta software. A toolchain-level Swift Concurrency runtime bug is a live possibility, not yet ruled out or in.
- Machine is persistently under heavy, unrelated load (load average 9-14 throughout this investigation, from other processes: multiple `rust-analyzer` instances, several `node` processes) — ruled out as the *sole* cause (CPU time on the hung process genuinely stops advancing, which pure contention/slowness would not produce), but can't be ruled out as a contributing/triggering factor for whatever the real bug is.

## Suggested next steps for whoever picks this up
- Consider a Thread-Sanitizer-instrumented build (`swift test --sanitize=thread`) if TSan support exists for this toolchain/platform — it's designed to catch exactly this class of bug and would likely pinpoint the exact unresolved continuation directly, far faster than further manual bisection.
- Bisection by suite subset (e.g. running the first N of ~30 suites, then N+5, etc.) to find the smallest reproducing combination, keeping in mind it may require several hundred tests' worth of *some* resource before manifesting (so small subsets may never reproduce it even if they contain the culprit).
- Audit every other `withTaskCancellationHandler`/`withCheckedContinuation`/`withTaskGroup` usage across `Sources/` and `Tests/Support/` (not just the `onCancel`-spawns-a-`Task` pattern already ruled out) for other structured-concurrency gaps.
- Try reproducing on a stable (non-beta) toolchain if one becomes available, to rule in/out a beta runtime bug.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Full `swift test` completes reliably (multiple consecutive clean runs) without hanging

 title: Full swift test suite still hangs (cumulative, unlocated) after fixing 2 confirmed leaks
 ---
 ## What
After fixing two confirmed, verified concurrency bugs this session (see `^332rkv2`: ConnectionTests' skipped `close()` on throw; and UpdateMailbox's actor/onCancel scheduling gap, fixed as part of `^a2pbwvz`'s still-uncommitted working tree), the full `swift test` suite **still hangs** on every attempted full run, but now much later and in a different, unrelated location each time.

## Evidence gathered
- 3x full runs at 300s timeout: hung at 419/~420 (right after ConnectionTests' own known flaky-timeout failure), 387/~420 (TSCallGraphTests), 389/~420 (CallGraphOpsTests/BlastRadiusOps area).
- 1x full run at 1200s (patient, no other timeout pressure): 388 tests passed, then hung — confirmed via two CPU-time samples 2 minutes apart showing **zero** change (0:07.36 both times) — genuinely stalled, not slow.
- 1x full run with `swift test --parallel --num-workers 1` at 900s: still hung, this time at 391/~420, in the StoreTests/SearchCorpus area. This suggests `--num-workers` (a SwiftPM multi-process/bundle-parallelism knob) does not control swift-testing's own internal per-test task concurrency within a single process, so it wasn't a useful lever here.
- Two separate `sample <pid> 3` thread dumps taken at two different hang points (once mid-CallGraphOpsTests-area, once mid-StoreTests-area) show the **identical signature**: only 1-2 threads alive total. Main thread parked in `mach_msg`/`CFRunLoopRun` (swift-testing's async-main driver loop, waiting for the top-level task to finish). One `libdispatch` worker thread parked in `__workq_kernreturn` (idle, waiting for work). **Nothing is blocked in a syscall, nothing is running** — this is not resource/thread-pool exhaustion via blocked reads (that was the ConnectionTests bug's signature: many threads alive, each pinned in a blocking `read(2)`). This is a genuinely un-resumed `CheckedContinuation`/`TaskGroup` child somewhere, causing the whole async-main task (and hence the entire remaining test run) to suspend forever.
- Never reproduces in any isolated suite: `DiagnosticsTests` alone (5/5 clean runs), `ConnectionTests` alone (repeated clean runs including the new regression test). Only manifests after several hundred tests have already run cleanly in the *same process*, and the exact location varies run to run — points to something cumulative/pervasive across many suites rather than one bad file.
- Grepped the entire `Sources/` tree for the same `actor` + detached-`Task`-inside-`onCancel` anti-pattern that caused the `UpdateMailbox` bug (see `^332rkv2`) — `UpdateMailbox` was the only instance, so this is not a second occurrence of that exact pattern.
- No stable (non-beta) Xcode/toolchain is available on this machine to compare against — this environment is Swift 6.4 / Xcode-beta.app targeting macOS 27.0 (26A5368g), all beta software. A toolchain-level Swift Concurrency runtime bug is a live possibility, not yet ruled out or in.
- Machine is persistently under heavy, unrelated load (load average 9-14 throughout this investigation, from other processes: multiple `rust-analyzer` instances, several `node` processes) — ruled out as the *sole* cause (CPU time on the hung process genuinely stops advancing, which pure contention/slowness would not produce), but can't be ruled out as a contributing/triggering factor for whatever the real bug is.

## Suggested next steps for whoever picks this up
- Consider a Thread-Sanitizer-instrumented build (`swift test --sanitize=thread`) if TSan support exists for this toolchain/platform — it's designed to catch exactly this class of bug and would likely pinpoint the exact unresolved continuation directly, far faster than further manual bisection.
- Bisection by suite subset (e.g. running the first N of ~30 suites, then N+5, etc.) to find the smallest reproducing combination, keeping in mind it may require several hundred tests' worth of *some* resource before manifesting (so small subsets may never reproduce it even if they contain the culprit).
- Audit every other `withTaskCancellationHandler`/`withCheckedContinuation`/`withTaskGroup` usage across `Sources/` and `Tests/Support/` (not just the `onCancel`-spawns-a-`Task` pattern already ruled out) for other structured-concurrency gaps.
- Try reproducing on a stable (non-beta) toolchain if one becomes available, to rule in/out a beta runtime bug.

## RESOLVED — see kanban comments for full investigation log
Three distinct root causes found and fixed:
1. `PendingRequestTable` (`Sources/FoundationModelsCodeContext/LSP/ProcessLanguageServerConnection.swift`) — a response could arrive and be silently discarded before its request's continuation finished registering. Fixed by buffering early responses (`resolveOrBuffer`). Committed as `038f3b6`.
2. `UpdateMailbox.registerWaiter` (`Sources/FoundationModelsCodeContext/Diagnostics/UpdateMailbox.swift`) — same class of bug: `record(_:)`/`markClosed()` could run in the gap between `next()`'s miss and registration, orphaning the continuation. Fixed by checking `!queued.isEmpty || isClosed` at registration time, mirroring `ManualClock`'s existing pattern. Committed as `581041f`.
3. `DiagnosticsTests.updateAtT200RestartsQuiescenceWindowSoSettleFiresAtT500NotT300()` — a test-harness synchronization gap (not a production bug): relied on a fixed real-time `Task.sleep` instead of a deterministic wait to know the settle loop had registered its restarted debounce deadline, which could fail under severe scheduler starvation. Fixed via new `ManualClock.waitForWaiter(withDeadline:)`. Uncommitted in working tree, ready for the orchestrator's commit step.

Verified via 4 consecutive full `swift test` runs (473 tests/37 suites) all completing in 6-38 seconds with only one pre-existing, unrelated failure (`DiagnosticsTests.scopeResolutionExcludesNonDiagnosableExtensions`, a git-scope-resolution issue with no connection to concurrency).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Full `swift test` completes reliably (multiple consecutive clean runs) without hanging
- [x] Root cause identified and documented, not just papered over
- [x] A regression test added if the root cause is in this package's own code (not the toolchain)

Filed from within task `^332rkv2`'s investigation; discovered while trying to verify tasks `^es8qv65` and `^a2pbwvz`'s work was safe to build on. Blocks confident full-suite verification for the rest of the `/finish` batch — the two isolated suites known to be involved in this session's other work (`DiagnosticsTests`, `ConnectionTests`) both pass reliably alone, so this is not blocking those two tasks specifically, but it does mean **no one should trust a full `swift test` run to actually complete** until this is resolved.
